// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 3 — PROMPTING AS SPECIFICATION

You send Claude a message: "Can you improve this introduction?"

Chapter 3 — Prompting as Specification — Two minutes later you have a rewritten paragraph. It is polished. The sentences are shorter. The word choice is cleaner.
NARRATION

You send Claude a message: "Can you improve this introduction?" Two minutes later you have a rewritten paragraph. It is polished. The sentences are shorter. The word choice is cleaner. It is also completely wrong for your audience, your journal, and the argument you are making , because Claude had no idea what "better" meant to you.

// SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.

·Identify the six components of a well-specified prompt
·Convert a vague request into an inspectable work order
·Use structure (plain headings or XML tags) to separate task, context, constraints, source material, output format, and e
·Diagnose a failing prompt before rewriting it
·Know when to ask Claude to elicit missing requirements before producing an answer
NARRATION

Identify the six components of a well-specified prompt Convert a vague request into an inspectable work order Use structure (plain headings or XML tags) to separate task, context, constraints, source material, output format, and evaluation criteria Diagnose a failing prompt before rewriting it Know when to ask Claude to elicit missing requirements before producing an answer

// SLIDE 03 — THE CORE CONCEPT: A PROMPT IS

THE CORE CONCEPT: A PROMPT IS A WORK ORDER

What to do→What to use→What to avoid→What the finished work should look like
NARRATION

The research community has spent years studying prompting as a family of techniques for adapting language models to tasks through input design (Liu, Yuan, Fu, Jiang, Hayashi, and Neubig 2023). The practitioner version of that insight is simpler: a prompt is a specification of work. A good specification tells the contractor , human or AI , four things:

// SLIDE 04 — THE SIX COMPONENTS OF A WELL-S

THE SIX COMPONENTS OF A WELL-SPECIFIED PROMPT.

Task — What should Claude do?
Context — What does Claude need to know?
Source material — What evidence should Claude use?
Constraints — What must Claude avoid?
NARRATION

Every effective prompt answers six questions. Not every prompt needs to answer all six in depth , a simple task may need only three. But knowing the six helps you diagnose why a prompt failed. The most commonly omitted components are source material, constraints, and evaluation criteria. When these are missing, Claude fills the gap with its best guess , which may be reasonable for a general reader and wrong for your specific situation.

// SLIDE 05 — WORKED WORKFLOW: REVISING A RE

WORKED WORKFLOW: REVISING A RESEARCH METHODS SECTION

A bullet-point diagnosis (issue, severityhigh/medium/low, reason).
·A prioritized revision plan (what to fix first and why).
·An optional revised opening paragraph only if the opening is unclear.
NARRATION

Here is how the same task looks before and after specification. "Can you improve the methods section of my paper?"

// SLIDE 06 — WHY XML? WHY NOT JUST WRITE IT

WHY XML? WHY NOT JUST WRITE IT OUT?.

You do not have to use XML tags. For a simple prompt with one or two components, plain prose or Markdown headings work f

Why XML? Why Not Just Write It Out? — XML tags become useful when your prompt has multiple sections and the model might confuse them (Anthropic, "Use XML tags
NARRATION

You do not have to use XML tags. For a simple prompt with one or two components, plain prose or Markdown headings work fine. XML tags become useful when your prompt has multiple sections and the model might confuse them (Anthropic, "Use XML tags to structure your prompts"). A long source document pasted after a long instruction, for example, can blur the line between the source and the task. XML tags make the boundary visible to Claude and to you.

// SLIDE 07 — THE REQUIREMENT-GATHERING CONV

THE REQUIREMENT-GATHERING CONVERSATION.

Not every task starts with a complete specification. Sometimes you do not know exactly what you need until you start. In

The Requirement-Gathering Conversation — "I need help evaluating a research methods section, but I am not sure what angle is most useful. Ask me three questions
NARRATION

Not every task starts with a complete specification. Sometimes you do not know exactly what you need until you start. In that case, the right first prompt is not a work order , it is a requirements conversation. "I need help evaluating a research methods section, but I am not sure what angle is most useful. Ask me three questions that will help you understand what I actually need, then propose a plan."

// SLIDE 08 — THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT TO VERIFY

THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT TO VERIFY

The purposewhy this work matters and what it will be used for
The source constraintswhat evidence is valid, what is not
The evaluation criteriawhat "good" means in your institutional, scientific, or professional context
The final decisionwhether the output is acceptable for use
·Knowledge of your organization's unstated norms
NARRATION

The specification frame clarifies what you still own. You must supply: The purpose , why this work matters and what it will be used for The source constraints , what evidence is valid, what is not The evaluation criteria , what "good" means in your institutional, scientific, or professional context The final decision , whether the output is acceptable for use

// SLIDE 09 — COMMON MISTAKES

COMMON MISTAKES

Asking for a finished artifact before asking for a diagnosis.→Using role prompts as a substitute for a task specification.→Treating XML or Markdown tags as magic.→Asking Claude to "make it better" without defining better.→Accepting a fluent output as a correct one.
NARRATION

Asking for a finished artifact before asking for a diagnosis. Request diagnosis and plan before the rewrite. It is easier to correct a plan than a completed draft. Using role prompts as a substitute for a task specification. "Act as an expert researcher" tells Claude to adopt a tone, not to do specific work. Role prompts can supplement a specification but should not replace it. Specify the task first; add role context if the register or perspective matters.

// SLIDE 10 — BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 4

BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 4.

Specification is the discipline that makes every other Claude workflow inspectable. The next chapter takes that discipli

NARRATION

Specification is the discipline that makes every other Claude workflow inspectable. The next chapter takes that discipline into a different environment: a codebase. Claude Code is not a chat assistant that writes code. It is an agent that operates on your files, runs commands, and requires a different kind of supervision. The specification habits from this chapter carry forward , but the verification tools change.

// SLIDE 11 — THESIS

THE CORE CONCEPT: A PROMPT IS A WORK ORD

The research community has spent years studying prompting as a family of techniques for adapting language models to task

A good specification tells the contractor — human or AI — four things:

NARRATION

The practitioner version of that insight is simpler: a prompt is a specification of work. A good specification tells the contractor , human or AI , four things:
